draft.introduction: The Tale of the Shipwrecked Sailor (c. 1900 BCE) is a frame narrative about a man cast ashore on a magical island ruled by a great serpent—the last survivor of a destroyed race of divine beings. The serpent delivers a prophecy of comfort before the island sinks.

translation.text: The serpent said to me: “Do not be afraid, do not be afraid, little one. Do not make your face pale. You have reached me. It is God who has let you live and brought you to this island of the ka. There is nothing that is not upon it. It is full of all good things.”
translation.original_text: ḏd(.jn) n=j pā ḥfāw: m snḏ, m snḏ, nḏs. m ḥwj=k ḥr=k. ḥār spḥ.n=k wj. nṬr pw rdj.n ānḫ=k

translator_notes.note: The serpent as a benevolent divine figure, the last survivor of a destroyed race, and the sinking magical island all suggest mythological traditions that may underlie later Greek accounts of Atlantis. The serpent’s consolation—“it is God who has let you live”—frames the entire adventure as providential.
